name: Brahman
shortDescription: In Buddhism, Brahman is understood differently than in Hinduism. While the term exists, it often refers to a high-ranking deva or is reinterpreted within the context of Buddhist philosophy.
description: Within Buddhist cosmology, the concept of Brahman is complex and nuanced, differing significantly from its Hindu counterpart. While the term "Brahman" appears in Buddhist texts, it does not represent the ultimate, unchanging reality or the source of all existence as it does in Hinduism. Instead, Brahman often refers to a specific class of deities, high-ranking devas residing in the higher realms of existence. These Brahmas, while powerful and long-lived, are still subject to the cycle of rebirth (samsara) and are not considered to be enlightened or free from suffering. Buddhist philosophy refutes the idea of a permanent, unchanging self or essence (atman) and, by extension, a universal Brahman from which all things originate. The Buddha challenged the Vedic understanding of Brahman, arguing that clinging to such notions leads to suffering and prevents the attainment of Nirvana. In some Buddhist contexts, the idea of Brahman is reinterpreted to align with Buddhist principles, such as emptiness (sunyata) and dependent origination. This reinterpretation often emphasizes the impermanence and interconnectedness of all phenomena, rather than a singular, absolute reality. Furthermore, the Buddhist texts often portray Brahmas as beings who can be misled or even deluded, demonstrating that even the highest deities are not immune to ignorance and the need for spiritual practice.

sources.title: The Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism
sources.author: Buswell, Robert E.; Lopez, Donald S.
